reasoning: The question asks whether a synthetic protein fiber (like synthetic spider silk or Spiber's Brewed Protein) will achieve a market price lower than petroleum-based nylon before 2030. Nylon is a widely used and highly optimized petrochemical commodity, generally costing around $2-$4 per kilogram. In contrast, producing synthetic protein fibers involves precision fermentation and costly downstream processing. Feedstock (e.g., sugars) and bioreactor limits make it fundamentally difficult for these bio-manufactured materials to compete with bulk petrochemicals on price. Current leaders in this space, such as Spiber and Bolt Threads, are explicitly targeting luxury markets (replacing cashmere, silk, and fine wool) because their production costs are roughly an order of magnitude higher (e.g., targeting $50-$100+/kg). Given that 2030 is just a few years away and these companies are barely reaching luxury commercialization, scaling up and optimizing costs by >10x to undercut nylon is extremely unlikely. Therefore, the probability is set to 2%, matching the community consensus. (Note: The resolution dates were parsed as "N", "/", "A", representing a single Jan 1, 2030 forecast, so 0.02 is repeated for each to satisfy the required array length).
